draft.introduction: The opening of Beowulf (c. 8th–10th century CE) establishes the heroic world of the Danes through the funeral of Scyld Scefing. His body is placed in a treasure-laden ship and sent out to sea—a scene of pagan grandeur preserved in a Christian manuscript.

translation.text: Listen! We have heard of the glory of the Spear-Danes in the old days, the kings of tribes, how those princes performed courageous deeds. Often Scyld Scefing seized mead-benches from many tribes, from troops of enemies, terrified warriors.
translation.original_text: Hwæt! Wē Gār-Dena in gēar-dagum, þēod-cyninga, þrym gefrūnon, hū ðā æþelingas ellen fremedon. Oft Scyld Scēfing sceaþena þrēatum, monegum mǣgþum, meodo-setla oftēah.

translator_notes.note: The opening word Hwæt (“Listen!”) is a conventional call to attention in Old English verse. The ship-funeral of Scyld, while described as a pagan rite, is preserved only in a Christian manuscript, illustrating the complex layering of traditions in the poem.
